input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A cyber‑physical building system that integrates Building Information Model (BIM) representations with runtime sensor, actuator and control data to form a virtual twin. The system comprises static architectural and MEP models, embedded physical devices (sensors, actuators, controllers), and a control infrastructure enabling real‑time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization across the building life cycle. Its suitability for twinning is driven by the need for fragmented BIM data to be dynamically synchronized, enabling data‑driven infrastructure management, reduced operational costs, and enhanced stakeholder decision making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers monitoring of building states, predictive maintenance alerts, optimization of energy usage, real‑time control of HVAC and lighting via integrated controllers, and immersive visualization interfaces for Facility Managers and Building Control Engineers. Services are exposed through a modular API, allowing DT Engineers to deploy custom analytics plugins and control logic extensions. Typical use cases include continuous data‑exchange based state synchronization, predictive analytics for fault detection, and real‑time command execution on physical devices.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework built atop enriched BIM models. Key enablers include: 1) BIM authoring tools to embed sensor, actuator and controller metadata; 2) Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to automatically translate enriched BIM into structured runtime models; 3) A runtime data‑exchange layer (protocol‑agnostic, using real‑time messaging) that synchronizes physical and digital states; 4) Modular extension points for custom DT services and analytics; and 5) Underpinning databases and simulation engines that feed the twin with real‑time operational data. The framework is applied in projects such as TwinLight and BOREALIS, showcasing its scalability across complex infrastructure environments.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The documented system is designed for real‑time data exchange between the physical building and its digital twin, providing continuous synchronization.  While explicit refresh rates or latency targets are not specified, the architecture supports near‑zero latency for monitoring and control through real‑time data pipelines, and runtime configuration allows adjusting update intervals to meet application‑specific service time‑scales and granularities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system is organized around a central DT Framework that acts as the hub for the building’s digital twin. This framework orchestrates the entire twin lifecycle by ingesting enriched BIM models and generating operational twin instances. It supports bidirectional, real‑time data exchange between the physical building and its digital counterpart, enabling continuous synchronization. The architecture is modular, exposing standardized extension points that allow DT engineers to integrate custom services and analytics without requiring a full redeployment. Runtime configuration management further enhances adaptability, enabling adjustments to the twin’s behavior on demand. Overall, the constellation provides a cohesive, centralized orchestration for monitoring, control, and service integration across the building life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation is founded on an enriched BIM model that contains geometric representations of the physical building and embedded device configurations (including photometric metadata for lighting). Model‑driven engineering (MDE) principles are applied to extract structured, semantic representations from this BIM artefact, generating deployable runtime components – TwinComponents for each device type. The computational models encompass geometric (BIM building layout), behavioral (control logic defined by the Building Control Engineer within the BIM), and analytical (policy‑based data validation rules encoded in Eclipse Ditto). Data sources include the BIM metadata, real‑time sensor and actuator telemetry, and configuration files. Data management is handled through Eclipse Ditto, which registers each device as a DittoDevice, enforces policy validation for value ranges and units, and persists events in a durable store. The runtime data flow follows: enriched BIM → MDE extraction → Ditto registry → Eclipse Hono message transport → UE 5 visualization. This pipeline ensures continuous bidirectional synchronization, automatic generation of runtime interfaces, and immersive interaction, while the modular host architecture allows additional services to be plugged in through defined extension points.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are instantiated directly in the BIM model by the Building Control Engineer and include lighting devices that expose an on/off switch, a brightness level, and a colour‑temperature parameter (derived from Revit photometric values). The remote control interface is provided by the UnrealTwinController, which brokers commands through the DittoController (REST/WS endpoints) and delivers them via Eclipse Hono’s MQTT transport to the physical device. Actuation ranges are governed by the photometric values stored in the BIM metadata, and safety constraints are enforced by pre‑execution checks in the DittoController that validate command payloads against defined policy ranges before dispatch. The communication channel guarantees at‑least‑once delivery, ensuring that commands are reliably propagated even in the face of transient network failures.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity and data integrity are maintained by policy‑based validation in Eclipse Ditto, which checks incoming sensor values against defined range and unit constraints and rejects out‑of‑range readings. Prior to executing actuator commands, the DittoController performs pre‑execution checks to prevent unsafe operations. Fault tolerance is achieved through Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, guaranteeing that no telemetry or command is lost. Continuous real‑time data exchange between the physical building and the Digital Twin serves as a runtime synchronization benchmark, validating that the twin state matches the on‑site state. While the documents do not specify quantitative uncertainty models, the described policy validations and persistent storage provide a robust quality‑assurance foundation for the Digital Twin.”}
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework implements real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronizes the physical building with its digital twin. Sensor observations collected from the physical assets (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, equipment status) are streamed to the DT using the framework’s bidirectional data exchange infrastructure. The data flow is continuous and managed by runtime configuration, allowing dynamic adjustment of transmission parameters without full redeployment. Event triggers for data upload include periodic sensor sampling and manual activation by facility managers. Pre‑processing is minimal; raw sensor values are forwarded to the DT, where they are integrated into the BIM‑based model via the framework’s standardized extension points. No specific data format, compression, or timestamp alignment mechanisms are detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control signals originating from the DT are sent back to the physical building control system (BCS). Commands such as HVAC set‑points, lighting schedules, or actuator controls are issued through the same data exchange infrastructure used for sensor data. The framework validates that the command aligns with the current BIM model before delivery, ensuring consistency between the virtual model and physical operations. Confirmation of command execution is received as feedback from the BCS, and any conflicts are handled by the operator or via the DT’s conflict resolution logic. Specific command message formats, authentication, or conflict resolution protocols are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Document 1 references real‑time data exchange and modular, extensible architecture but does not detail the underlying network infrastructure.  The framework’s scalability and interoperability requirements (see Section II of Document 1) imply the use of a publish/subscribe middleware capable of supporting low‑latency, high‑throughput messaging across heterogeneous systems.  Specific protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency guarantees, or security mechanisms are not explicitly described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports four main lifecycle stages: (1) **Device Modeling** – Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with explicit sensor, actuator, and controller descriptions (Definition of BCS components) to overcome BIM’s static representation limitation; (2) **Model Transformation** – Applying Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles, the enriched BIM is automatically parsed into a structured, deployable Digital Twin artifact, managing the transition from static architectural representation to a dynamic runtime model; (3) **Deployment & Runtime Operation** – The generated DT is deployed to the edge or cloud, with real‑time data exchange protocols maintaining continuous synchronization with the physical building; Facility Managers then monitor and interact with the twin via interactive visualization interfaces; (4) **Service Extension & Governance** – A modular architecture with standardized extension points allows DT Engineers to integrate custom analytics or control plugins, enabling governance of evolution and reduced manual configuration during any lifecycle transition.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Development follows a model‑driven workflow: enriched BIM models are automatically transformed using MDE techniques into deployable DT services with minimal manual intervention; the process incorporates real‑time data exchange mechanisms for continuous synchronization and interactive visualization for validation; custom services are appended through standardized extension points, facilitating version control at the component level and evolution management without redeploying the entire twin; quality assurance is implicitly maintained by the consistency of the automated mapping from BIM to the live model, while the modular design supports iterative updates and integration of additional analytics or control logic by DT Engineers.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Facility Managers act as the primary decision‑makers, utilizing the twin’s real‑time data stream and interactive visualization to monitor building conditions and to issue control actions directly on the physical infrastructure. The system provides a unified view of sensor readings, actuator states, and control logic feedback, enabling managers to assess operational status and coordinate maintenance or optimization activities. No automated predictive analytics or confidence metrics are specified within the described framework; decision support is based on direct observation and manual interaction with the twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework is built upon the BIM semantic backbone, extending standard Building Information Modeling (BIM) representations (e.g., IFC) with device‑level annotations for sensors, actuators, and controllers. It removes fragmented, case‑specific data schemas by generating a unified, machine‑readable model from enriched BIM, thereby ensuring interoperability across stakeholders. The solution adheres to BIM standards for data exchange and adopts a modular architecture with standardized extension points to support additional services, but does not rely on external communication protocols beyond the real‑time data exchange mechanisms referenced in the literature. No specific industry certification or compliance frameworks are cited in the provided documents.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately captures the definition and purpose of the system as a cyber-physical building system that integrates BIM with runtime data. It correctly identifies the main components (static models, embedded devices) and the motivations (synchronization, reduced costs, decision making) described in the text. Quotes: 'Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static models with runtime data creates a virtual representation', 'DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management and optimization... reduced operational costs, enhanced decision-making'.

The extraction correctly identifies key services such as monitoring, optimization, and visualization, as well as the user roles (Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and the modular architecture. However, it adds specific details not found in the source text, such as 'predictive maintenance alerts' (text only mentions predictive analytics), 'fault detection', and 'HVAC' (text specifically mentions lighting projects but not HVAC). Quotes: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate list of the core technologies, including the model-driven framework, BIM tools, MDE principles, and specific middleware (Eclipse Hono and Ditto). It also correctly identifies the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects. However, it adds some unsupported generic details, such as 'protocol-agnostic', 'Underpinning databases', and suggests simulation engines feed real-time operational data, whereas the text attributes real-time data exchange to Hono/Ditto. Quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the high-level architecture described in the abstract and introduction, including the use of BIM, MDE principles, Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine. However, it includes specific implementation details such as 'TwinComponents', 'DittoDevice', and specific data flow steps (e.g., 'Ditto registry') which are not present in the provided source text. The text supports the general framework but lacks these specific technical artifacts.

The extraction correctly identifies that the system involves lighting actuators modeled within BIM by the Building Control Engineer, supported by the mention of 'TwinLight' and 'control semantics for lighting systems'. However, it adds unsupported technical details such as specific parameters (color-temperature), controller names (UnrealTwinController, DittoController), and transport protocols (MQTT) which are not found in the source text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions the capability to define sensors within the framework. Table III states that the requirements allow 'building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', proving relevant information exists.

The extraction claims 'documents do not specify quantitative uncertainty models' and describes validation mechanisms using Eclipse Ditto that are not in the source text. Conversely, it ignores the explicit fidelity metric mentioned in the source: 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'. The extraction misrepresents the content of the documents.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

The mention of 'manual activation' as an event trigger is also speculative.

It also speculates about command validation against BIM models, feedback confirmation, and conflict resolution logic, which are absent from the source documents.

The extraction accurately assesses the scope of information in the source text. It correctly states that the document references 'real-time data exchange' and implies middleware (Eclipse Hono and Ditto) but truthfully notes that 'Specific protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency guarantees, or security mechanisms are not explicitly described' in the provided snippets.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', which is accurate regarding the specific definition of hosting infrastructure. While the document discusses a 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment' and an 'automated deployment process' (Abstract, R2), it focuses on the software engineering methodology (MDE) rather than the hosting environment details. The text does not mention 'hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise)', 'computational and storage requirements', or specific 'deployment models supported'.

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
